abstract: Single-crystal X-ray diffraction is used to re-examine the crystal structure of the high-mobility semimetal Cd₃As₂, a material recently identified as a three-dimensional Dirac semimetal. Contrary to previous reports of a noncentrosymmetric lattice, this study finds that the low-temperature phase is centrosymmetric, crystallizing in the $I4_1/acd$ space group with unit cell parameters $a = 12.633(3)$ Å and $c = 25.427(7)$ Å. The structure is an ordered derivative of the antifluorite type, characterized by distorted Cd₆$ \square _2$ cubes (where $\square$ represents a vacancy) that form columns of opposing chirality. Electronic structure calculations based on this centrosymmetric model reveal a four-fold degenerate Dirac point along the $\Gamma$-Z line at the Fermi level. Because inversion symmetry forbids spin splitting, all bands remain spin-degenerate, establishing Cd₃As₂ as a true 3D electronic analog to graphene. Additionally, scanning tunneling microscopy of the (112) cleavage plane reveals a $2 \times 2$ surface reconstruction, and the preferred growth direction of needle-like crystals is determined to be the [110] axis.
